77.title: Creating a Character:
77.content: . | Print Out a Character Sheet, or draw your own on a sheet of paper. | Roll 3d6 for each of the three stats, STRength, DEXterity, WILLpower, in order. After you have rolled, you may then pick two of the results and swap them, if you’d like. | Characters start with d6 Hit Protection, representing their ability to avoid taking serious damage. | Characters start with 2 x d6 New Pounds (£), enough to buy a couple sandwiches, or varying quality. | You had a shot at a normal life and you blew it. Match your highest Attribute with your lowest to determine your Failed Career. Your Hit Protection and Starting Currency determine further features related to your failed career. | Choose a Name for your character. Some folks have one name, others have multiple. Some have nicknames, aliases, family names, or village names. Some names come with descripters, sometimes accurate, and sometimes false (Bujamew the Magnanimous, Fragrant Joe, etc). Some names describe a character’s class (Agu the Money Lender, Bilbo the Burglar, etc). Take inspiration from the names associated with your Failed Career, or more general Tables of names. | Choose some Character Traits to distinguish your character, or Roll to give you inspiration. | .

78.title: Further Character Development
78.content: When a session concludes, answer one of the following questions: . | Why did your character become imprisoned. What prompted them to subject themselves to the bondage contract of the Martel Trading Company? In a future session, if you take a short rest to tell others from the crew about this story, you may add one to a relevant attribute. | What does your character value the most, and what has it cost you so far to maintain that value? Use the answer to this question to come up with a Core Belief your character might hold. Bonus points if this value is something that could come into conflict with the values of the other characters in your crew. | Think of a time your adventurer struggled with something while they were young, that they are still ashamed of today. When you admit your shame to another player during a short rest, you may add one to an attribute relevant to the story. | . In addition, you may also form a new Connection with a character from the session you’ve just played. This connection may be related to events between the characters in the present, or a past before our campaign (feel free to invent past events, provided that the other character agrees). Describe your connection, perhaps in reference from one of several Archetypes. Connections are not fixed in stone. In subsequent sessions, Connections between characters may change. At the end of each session, discuss with other players if you feel your interactions in game have strengthened, weakened, or changed completely the relationship with one another. Once each question has been answered, and you’ve formed at least three connections with other characters, add d4 to your Hit Protection.

95.title: Core Principles
95.content: These rules provide guidelines for the Steward and other Players, to help foster a specific play experience defined by critical thinking, engagement with the imaginary world, character development, and emergent narrative. Roles . Chime challenges the Steward to act as a neutral arbiter, portraying grounded situations and non-player characters to the other players, and providing contextual clarity to foster the other Players ability to make interesting decisions. Chime challenges the other Players to engage with the imaginary world, portray interesting characters with strong beliefs, respond to situations in clever and interesting ways, and to drive the fiction forward in interesting ways through their decisions. Fiction First . In world context overrides mechanics and numbers. Players engage with the world by describing their character’s actions within the world, with the consequences of those actions arbitrated by the Steward rather than mediated through contextual rules in a rulebook. This is closely related to the maxim of ‘Rulings Not Rules’ oft discussed in the gaming community. Classless . A Character’s role or skills is not defined by a particular class or attributes. Instead, a character specializes with their experience, their learning, and the equipment they choose to carry. Danger . Characters may be powerful, but they are also vulnerable to harm in its many forms. Death is always around the corner, but it should never be random or without warning. A dagger to your throat will kill a man, regardless of their expensive armor and impressive training. This danger adds impact to a Player’s decisions, and forces Players to engage with the imaginary world, as your character is not superheroic enough to be ‘above’ the world. Growth . Characters are changed through in-world advancement, gaining new skills and abilities by surviving dangerous events, overcoming obstacles, and making connections with others within the imaginary world. Player Choice . Players should always understand the reasons behind the choices they’ve made, and information about potential risks should be provided freely and frequently by the Steward. Emergent Narrative . Neither the Steward nor the other Players are responsible for moulding a narrative structure from the game. Players act according to beliefs, motivations, and their perception of the imaginary world, and the Steward provides a lens to the imaginary world, as well as fostering the Player’s ability to make interesting decisions. Narrative emerges from these interesting decisions, just as stories emerge in real life, i.e. by filtering through a series of events to find a chain of interconnected events that have meaning to us.

96.title: Player Guidelines
96.content: Agency . | Attributes are tools to engage with the imaginary world, but do not define your character. | Engage with the imaginary world as if you existed within it. | Be creative with your intuitions, items, and connections. The game challenges your own ability to think outside the box as well as your ability to embody a character within the imaginary world. The main appeal of a role playing game over video games or board games is that you can take any actions within the imaginary world, with the Steward there to interpret the consequences. Try and make the Steward say “I didn’t even think of that!”. | . Teamwork . | Trust each other to engage with the shared setting, to help others with the goals of their characters and of the overall team. Work together as a team. | Seek consensus from other players before barreling forward. | Stay on the same page about goals and limits, respecting each other and accomplishing more as a group than alone. | Let the relationships between your characters grow and change. Every session or two, discuss with how your characters feel about each other in light of your most recent experiences. Have rivals become allies? Have allies become best friends? Have best friends become lovers? | . Engage With The World . | Asking questions, listening to details, and engaging with the world is more useful than any stats, items, or skills you have. | Take the Steward’s description without suspicion, but do not shy away from scrutinizing your environment for more information. | There is no correct solution to any problem, nor any single correct path forward. | . Talking . | Treat NPCs as if they were real people, and rely on your curiosity to safely gain information and solve problems. | You’ll find that most people are interesting and will want to talk things through before getting violent. | . Caution . | Think of ways to avoid your obstacles through reconnaissance, subtlety, and information gathering. | Fighting is a choice, and rarely a wise one; consider whether violence is the best way to achieve your goals. | Stack the odds in your favor as much as possible, and always keep retreat as an option when a situation becomes unfavorable. | . Ambition . | Set goals for your characters, and use your meager means to accomplish them. | Embody characters with beliefs that distinguish them from the general party. Set personal goals to develop your character further. | Actively move situations forward when they stall, plan goals for sessions in advance to keep gaes focused, and play to find out what happens rather than anticipating plot threads or expecting certain outcomes. | .

97.title: Steward Guidelines
97.content: Information . | Provide useful information in response to the explorations of the Players. Respond to smart questions with helpful, direct, consistent answers. | Players do not need to roll dice to understand their circumstances. | . Difficulty . | If something the players want to do is sincerely impossible, no roll will allow them to do it. | If a player leverages a situation in a sensible, risk-free manner, let the consequences happen without a roll. | Saves cover many uncertain situations and are often all that is necessary for risky actions. | . Preparation . | Use random tables and generators to develop situations, not stories or plots. | NPCs remember what the PCs say and do, and how they affect the world. | NPCs don’t want to die. Infuse their own self-interest and will to live into every personality. | . Narrative Focus . | Emergent play matters more than a planned outcome. | Give the players opportunities to facilitate improvement and specialization, and encourage them to foster personal goals. | Pay attention to the desires and beliefs of the players, then put realistic opportunities in their path. | . Danger . | The game world produces real risk of pain and death for the player characters. | Telegraph serious danger to players when it is present. The more dangerous, the more obvious. Put traps in plain sight. | Give players opportunities to solve problems and interact with the world. | . Treasure . | Treasures are specific to the environment from where it is recovered. They tell a story. | Treasure are highly valuable, almost always bulky, and rarely useful beyond its worth and prestige. | Relics are not Treasure, though they are useful and interesting. | Use Treasure as a lure to exotic locations under the protection of intimidating foes. | . Choice . | Give players solid choices to force outcomes when situations lull. | Ensure that the player character’s actions are impactful, leaving a mark on the imaginary world. | .

100.title: Attributes
100.content: Player Characters (PCs) have three Attributes: . | Strength (STR): Used for Saves requiring physical power, like lifting gates, bending bars, resisting poison, etc. Dead if 0. | Dexterity (DEX): Used for Saves requiring poise, speed, reflexes, dodging, climbing, sneaking, balancing, etc. Paralyzed if 0. | Willpower (WIL): Used for Saves to persuade, deceive, interrogate, intimidate, charm, provoke, manipulate spells, etc. Mentally broken if 0. | . Attributes are not universal descripters of a character’s abilities. A character with low STR is not necessarily hopelessly weak, but in risky, tense situations their strength is more likely to fail them.

101.title: Hit Protection
101.content: . | Hit Protection (HP) reflects a character’s ability to avoid serious damage in combat. | This measurement does not indicate a character’s health or fortitude, nor do they lose it for very long. See Healing & Recovery. | Hit Protection only applies in combat. If a PC takes damage outside of combat, it should be taken from an Attribute, typically STR. | .

102.title: Healing & Recovery
102.content: . | Resting for a few minutes and having a drink of water restores lost HP but may leave the party exposed. | Water is kept in a Waterskin, which occupies 1 slot. Fully filled it has a Usage Die of d8. Whenever a character recovers HP, they roll the waterskin’s Usage Die. On a 1 or 2, the die decreases in size (d8>d6>d4). On a 1-2 on a d4, the waterskin is empty. | During a rest, players are encouraged to role-play a scene that develops the fellowship between the party. Share a memory, talk about goals, ask about their history or tell them yours. | Players then nominate the party member whose fellowship was most brought up during the rest gain a Bond which lasts until the next time the players rest (and so only a single player has a Bond at any moment of play); if that player makes a Save they feel relates to the conversation during the last rest, they may spend their Bond to gain advantage on the roll. | . | Bandages can stabilize a character that has taken Critical Damage, though the character will normally be Deprived until recovered. | Attribute Loss can usually be restored with a week’s rest, facilitated by a healer or other appropriate source of expertise. | Some healing services are free, while magical or more expedient means of recovery may come at a cost. | .

103.title: Wounds
103.content: Some types of damage can leave Wounds, which hinder affected adventurers more than simply dealing damage. Fire can inflict burns, electricity can inflict nerve damage, cold can inflict frostbite, monsters can inflict wounds upon dealing Critical Damage, etc. A Wound occupies a slot in the adventurer’s inventory. Wounds come in 3 levels. Whenever a wound of the same type is gained, the player can choose to have their existing wound increase in severity or to fill in a new slot in their inventory. This allows player choice between short or long-term impact: do you suffer another light wound and heal after combat or have the old wound increase in severity without taking up another slot but possibly leading to permanent injury? . | Light Wounds can be healed in the field if proper medicine and treatment are available. Light burns can be healed by gathering the right herbs and applying them overnight. | Severe Wounds require serious medical attention, probably between adventures. Severe frostbite could be cured with a comfortable week-long rest in a healing temple. | Permanent Wounds can never be healed. Every Permanent Wound also lowers a fitting Attribute by -1 (For instance, WIL for nerve damage). While this -1 penalty can be overcome in time by training, the wound’s Inventory slot is forever lost. | . [//]: # “Comment” Wounds are intended to provide a prompt for adventure. As an example, Burns can be healed by applying bandages soaked in a mixture of water and Saint’s Hood, a white flower often found on ground once charred by forest fires. Frostbite can be healed with a salve of firevine peppers. Nerve damage can be healed by chewing on a steady supply of Silverleaf, a fragile herb that grows on trees struck by lightning. Poisoning can be healed by brewing tea from Foxglove, a purple flower that grows near small streams.

104.title: Armor
104.content: . | Before calculating HP Damage, subtract the target’s Armor Protection (AP) value from the result. | Shields and similar armor provide a bonus to AP while worn. Some may also provide additional benefits, depending on the fiction. | No Character, PC or otherwise, can have more than 3 AP. | .

105.title: Inventory
105.content: . | A characters Inventory represents both their literal equipment load, as well as a kind of ‘stamina bar’, throught eh Fatigue system. | Inventory is abstract, dependent only on the fiction as adjudicated by the Steward. Anyone carrying more than 10 items is reduced to 0 HP and takes all Saves which may be affected by overencumbrance with disadvantage. Anyone carrying at most five items has a Light Load, and gains advantage on all Saves which may be advantaged by being more light on their feet. | Horses, mules, carts, and hirelings can make a huge difference in how much a PC can bring with them on an adventure, though these options may bring with them their own problems. | As a group, your crew may always assume they are carrying torches, rope, food, water, and camping equipment, and these items do not take up inventory slots, unless the lack of these items becomes important to the fiction. Any other items should be explicitly written in your inventory. | . Inventory Slots . | Most items take up one slot unless otherwise indicated. | Petty items do not take up any slots. Bulky items take up two slots. | A bag of coins worth less than £100 is Petty. | .

106.title: Deprivation & Fatigue
106.content: . | A PC that lacks a crucial need (such as food or rest) is Deprived. Anyone Deprived for more than a day adds Fatigue to their inventory, one for each day. A Deprived PC cannot recover HP, cannot recover Attributes, and cannot recover item slots from Fatigue until they get rid of their Deprived Status. | A PC may also be forced to add Fatigue after casting spells or due to events in the fiction. Each Fatigue occupies one inventory slot and lasts until the PC is able to recuperate (i.e. after a full night’s rest in a safe spot). | .

107.title: Saves & Risk
107.content: . | A Save is a roll to avoid negative outcomes thrust upon the players outside of their volition. Saves are typically used for moments of split-second decisions, when something suddenly happens to a character. | Characters roll a d20 and compare the results to the appropriate attribute. If they roll equal to or under that attribute, they succeed. Otherwise, they fail. A 1 is always a success, and a 20 is always a failure. | Characters should be universally encouraged to plot, scheme, prepare, and manipulate a situation to prevent die rolls. After all, a dice roll means an opportunity of failure. | If two opponents are each trying to overcome the other, whoever is most at risk should save. In the case this cannot be decided, make a Contested Roll. Both Characters make a roll in a relevant attribute, and the highest successful roll wins. In the case that both rolls fail, the situation gets worse for both participants. | If two characters need to take an action together, whoever is most at risk should save (usually the character with the lowest relevant Attribute). | .

108.title: Time, Gear, Skill
108.content: When a character consciously performs an Action that is neither impossible, nor without chance of failure, use the Time, Gear & Skill system: . | If a Character is not explicitly in a rush, and they have time to calmly assess a situation and pick a best course of action, then they have Time. | If a Character has gear, equipment, or environmental benefits that outweigh the hindrences from such, then they have Gear. | If a Character has explicit skills, background specialization in a task, then they have Skill. | . If a Character has Time, Gear, and Skill, then they automatically succeed in an Action, with no need to roll. If a character only has one, or none of the three, then they automatically fail, with non need to roll. If Characters have two of the three, roll 1d6: On a 1, they fail, and the situation changes. On a 2-3, they Succeed at a Severe Cost (an item breaks, they gain Fatigue and take damage, etc), on a 4-6 they Succeed. Consider the following examples: . When Lockpicking, a character has Time if they have ten minutes to inspect the lock and fiddle with it, Gear if they have lockpicks, and Skill if they are a thief, or if they have 15+ DEX. When Sneaking, a character has Time if they’re not being actively chased, Gear if they’re wearing soft-soled boots (and no heavy armor, or are carrying less than 5 items), and Skill if they’re trained to be stealthy, or have 15+ DEX. When Smashing a Door Open, a character has Time if they’re not actively under attack, Gear if they have an axe, or a crowbar, and Skill if they were once a firefighter. When Climbing a Wall, a character has Time if they’re not being chased, Gear if they’ve got a rope and pitons (and no heavy armor), and Skill if they were once an Acrobat. When Plotting an escape route through the city, a party have Time if they’re planning it well in advance, Gear if they have pencil and paper to note down guard routes, and good connection with the locals, and Skill if they’re a thief, or they have good familiarity with the city.

109.title: Magic
109.content: Spellbooks . | Spellbooks contain a single spell and take up one Inventory Slot. They cannot be easily transcribed or created; instead they are recovered from places like tombs, dungeons, and manors. | Spellbooks sometimes display unusual properties or limitations, such as producing a foul or unearthly smell when opened, possessing an innate intelligence, or being legible only when held in moonlight. | Spellbooks will attract the attention of those who seek the arcane power within, and it is considered dangerous to display them openly. | . Casting Spells . | Anyone can cast a spell by holding a Spellbook in both hands and reading its contents aloud. They must then take a Fatigue. | Given time and safety, PCs can enhance a spell’s impact (affecting multiple targets, increasing its power, etc.) without additional cost. | If the PC is Deprived or in danger (such as during combat), the Steward may require a PC to make a WIL save to avoid any ill-effects from casting the spell. Consequences of failure are on par with the intended effect and may result in additional Fatigue, the destruction of the Spellbook, injury, and even death. | . Scrolls . Scrolls are similar to Spellbooks, however: . | They are Petty. | They do not cause Fatigue. | They disappear after one use. | . Relics . Relics are items imbued with magical power. They do not cause Fatigue. Relics usually have limited use, as well as a Recharge condition.

110.title: Non-Player Characters
110.content: Hirelings . | Adventuring parties can recruit Hirelings, in places depending on the fiction, relying on their unique skills, knowledge, and training to aid in expeditions. | To create a Hireling, choose an appropriate role from the Hirelings table. Roll 3d6 for each attribute and 1d6 for their HP. Give them equipment appropriate to their station, then roll on the Character Traits tables to further flesh them out. | .

111.title: Combat
111.content: Rounds . | A Round is roughly ten seconds of in-game time and proceeds with each side taking Turns. Each Round starts with any PC that is able to act, followed by their opponents. Each player declares the actions of their characters, before simultaneously resolving their actions. | During the first Round of combat, each PC must make a DEX Save in order to act. Special circumstances, abilities, items, or skills may negate this requirement. PCs that fail their save lose their Turn for this round. | After the PC’s first round, their opponents then take their turn, and the first round ends. The next round begins with the PCs taking their turn, followed by their opponents, and so on until combat has ended with one side defeated or fled. | . Actions . On their Turn, a Character may move up to 40ft and take up to one Action. This can be anything reasonably done in 10 seconds, such as casting a spell, attacking, or moving for a second time. Each round, all PCs declare what they are doing before dice are rolled. If a character attempts something risky, the Steward calls for an appropriate save. Attacking & Damage . | Attacks in combat automatically hit. The attacker rolls their damage die and subtracts the target’s armor, then deals the remaining total to their opponent’s HP. | If multiple attackers target the same foe, roll all damage dice and keep the single highest result. All actions are declared before being resolved. | If an attack would take a PC’s HP exactly to 0, refer to the Scars table to see how they are uniquely impacted. | . Attack Modifiers . | If fighting from a position of weakness (such as through cover or with bound hands), the attack is Impaired, and the attacker must roll d4 damage regardless of their damage die. Unarmed attacks always do d4 damage. | If fighting from a position of advantage (such as against a helpless foe or through a daring maneuver), the attack is Enhanced, allowing the attacker to roll d12 damage instead of their normal die. | Attacks with the Blast quality damage all targets in the noted area, rolling separately for each affected character. This can be anything from explosions to a dragon’s breath or the impact of a meteorite. If unsure how many targets can be affected, roll the related damage die for a result. | If attacking with two weapons at the same time, roll both damage dice and keep the single highest result (denoted with a plus symbol, e.g. d8+d8). | . Critical Damage . | Damage that reduces a target’s HP below zero is subtracted from their STR by the amount of damage remaining. The target must then immediately make a STR save to avoid taking Critical Damage, using their new STR score. On a success, the target is still in the fight (albeit with a lower STR score) and must continue to make critical damage saves when incurring damage. | Any PC that suffers Critical Damage cannot do anything but crawl weakly, grasping for life. If given aid (such as bandages), they will stabilize, typically bearing Wounds from their Critical Damage. If left untreated, they die within the hour. NPCs and monsters that fail a Critical Damage Save are considered dead, per the Steward’s discretion. Additionally, some enemies will have special abilities or effects that are triggered when their target fails a critical damage save. | . Character Death . | When a character dies, the player should create a new Character or take control of a Hireling. They immediately join the party in order to reduce time sat out of the game. | . Detachments . | Large groups of similar combatants fighting together are treated as a single Detachment. When a Detachment takes Critical Damage, it is routed or significantly weakened. When it reaches 0 STR, it is destroyed. | Attacks against Detachments by individuals are Impaired (excluding Blast damage). Attacks against individuals by detachments are Enhanced and deal Blast damage. | . Retreat . | Running away from a dire situation always requires a successful DEX Save, as well as a safe destination to run to. | . Morale . | Enemies must pass a WIL Save to avoid fleeing when they take their first casualty and again when they lose half their number. Lone foes must save when they’re reduced to 0 HP. | Some groups may use their leader’s WIL in place of their own. | Morale does not affect PCs. | . Ranged Attacks . | Ranged weapons can target any enemy near enough to see the whites of their eyes. Attacks against especially distant targets are Impaired. | Ammunition is not tracked unless otherwise specified. | . Scars . If damage to a PC would reduce their HP to exactly 0, look up the result on the table below based on the amount of HP lost in the attack. For example, if a PC went from 3 HP to 0 HP, they would look at entry #3 (Walloped). |   |   | . | HP Lost | Result | . | 1 | Lasting Scar: Roll 1d6. 1: Neck, 2: Hands, 3: Eye, 4: Chest, 5: Legs, 6: Ear. Roll 1d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 2 | Rattling Blow: You’re disoriented and shaken. Describe how you refocus. Roll 1d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 3 | Walloped: You’re sent flying and land flat on your face, winded. You are deprived until you rest for a few hours. Then, roll 1d6. Add that amount to your max HP. | . | 4 | Broken Limb: Roll 1d6. 1-2: Leg, 3-4: Arm, 5: Rib, 6: Skull. Once mended, roll 2d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 5 | Diseased: You’re afflicted with a gross, uncomfortable infection. When you get over it, roll 2d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | . | 6 | Reorienting Head Wound: Roll 1d6. 1-2: STR, 3-4: DEX, 5-6: WIL. Roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your current attribute, take the new result. | . | 7 | Hamstrung: You can barely move until you get serious help and rest. After recovery, roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max DEX, take the new result. | . | 8 | Deafened: You cannot hear anything until you find extraordinary aid. Regardless, make a WIL save. If you pass, increase your max WIL by 1d4. | . | 9 | Re-brained: Some hidden part of your psyche is knocked loose. Roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max WIL, take the new result. | . | 10 | Sundered: An appendage is torn off, crippled, or useless. (The Steward will tell you which.) Then make a WIL save. If you pass, increase your max WIL by 1d6. | . | 11 | Mortal Wound: You are deprived and out of action. You die in one hour unless healed. Upon recovery, roll 2d6. Take the new result as your max HP. | . | 12 | Doomed: Death seemed ever so close, but somehow you survived. If your next save against critical damage is a fail, you die horribly. If you pass, roll 3d6. If the total is higher than your max HP, take the new result. | .

254.title: Home
254.content: A year ago they took you. Black bagged, condemned to a cell. It wasn’t fair. It didn’t matter. Six months passed, and a woman immaculate and dangerous as a polished shiv offered you a deal. The Martel Company needs recruits. The work will be hard. Dangerous. But you can buy back your name. In time. For six months you hauled rocks. Big stones sunk deep in the mud. Knotburns buried deep into your palms. You were not alone. They put you on a ship, and the crew became your world. You slept together, bathed together, ate together, shat together. Maybe you’ve become friends. Maybe you hate each others guts. But you trust them, because you must. They branded your left shoulder when the ship set sail. Black magic swirled around the iron, felt like ice when you were marked. They told you that you belong to the Martel now, and that your job is to haul. Gold. Statues. Art. The more you haul, the quicker your brand will fade. You’re free to run. But in time, the brand will consume your flesh, sloughed off like corn husk. And so with your crew, they sailed you to the Swordfish Islands… . Chime is an adventure game about exploring a dark and mysterious island of primal forces, life, and abundance, mainly a hack of Cairn, divined by the Haruspex of Hotsprings Island from the entrails of: . | Block, Dodge, Parry! | Electric Bastionland. | His Majesty the Worm. | . Character generation is quick and random, adventures are tense and reward careful exploration, and combat is frantic and deadly.

422.title: Ally
422.content: You are professional and amiable to your crew-mate. This is probably the default archetype. Examples: . | Wash and Kaylee. | Aragorn and Gandalf. | .

423.title: Adversary
423.content: If you have a significant problem with a crew-mate, you’re probably their adversary. This isn’t an excuse to be an asshole to other Players in the game, but it’s an opportunity for interesting and fair conflict within the game. Examples: . | Mal and Jayne. | Boromir and Aragorn. | .

424.title: Best Friend
424.content: You wouldn’t let your best friend go on an adventure without you. You’re there for each other, through thick and thin. Examples: . | Mal and Zoe. | Merry and Pippin. | .

425.title: Love
425.content: If both players are cool with it, two characters may be romantically interested in one another. If the relationship is one sided, Unrequited Love may be more applicable. Examples: . | Zoe and Wash. | Faramir and Éowyn. | .

426.title: Master / Henchmen
426.content: When you have a managerial vs. subservient relationship with another crew-mate, you probably fit a Master / Henchmen archetype. Examples: . | Mal and Wash. | Frodo and Sam. | .

427.title: Mentor / Mentee
427.content: When a parent, parent-figure, or mentor guies and shapes the life of a mentee, they fit the Mentor / Mentee archetype. Examples: . | Shepherd Book and River. | Gandalf and Frodo. | .

428.title: Rival
428.content: You are in competition with your rival. You might want the same woman, the most kills, or something weirder. Examples: . | Legolas and Gimli. | .

429.title: Sibling

430.title: Unrequited Love
